data.title: Gingrich: ‘Republicans are in real trouble’ if economy doesn’t ‘recover’
data.description:
Former Speaker Newt Gingrich (R-Ga.) said Thursday that the GOP is “in real trouble” if the economy does not “recover.”

“Look, I think it’s pretty straightforward. If the economy recovers, as you and I think it will, Republicans will keep the House and increase their margin in the Senate,” Gingrich told Fox Business Network’s Larry Kudlow on “Kudlow.”

“If the economy does not recover, Republicans are in real trouble, and I think the president’s goal has to be to continue to cut spending, to continue to cut regulations, to allow the One Big, beautiful Bill to go into effect January 1 and to continue, around the world, marketing investment in the U.S. and purchase of American goods,” he added.

Republicans lawmakers’ concerns have been recently piqued over signals next year’s midterm elections might be a landslide against the GOP that could result in them losing control of the House and having their Senate majority shrink.

Senate Republicans previously said the off-year elections in New Jersey, Virginia and other American regions in early November were a wake-up call and advised that President Trump and GOP congressional leaders had to confront worries over the slowing economy and high costs.

But Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) said Thursday he is “very, very bullish about the midterms.”

“I know that we are going to win because we have a great record to run on,” the Louisiana Republican told The Hill. “We have better candidates, better message and we have a lot to show the people, so we’ll continue our momentum.”

The House Democrats’ campaign arm recently put five more races on its offensive battleground target list for next year, with recent election victories making the party feel more confident about the 2026 midterms.

data.title: Meet the candidates running to be the next U.S. Senator from Texas: James Talarico
data.description:
AUSTIN (Nexstar) — The U.S. Senate race in Texas is quickly shaping up to be one of the most contentious elections across the United States in the 2026 cycle. Since 2003, the seat has belonged to Sen. John Cornyn, R-Texas, who is aiming for a fifth term.

State Rep. James Talarico, D-Round Rock, started his political career in 2018 when he flipped a Texas House seat from Republican to Democratic in a special election to replace retiring State Rep. Larry Gonzales. 

Talarico often cites his experience as a teacher at Rhodes Middle School in San Antonio as his reason for running. According to Census Reporter, 42.7% of people who reside in Rhodes’ zip code live below the poverty line.

Nexstar State Capitol Correspondent Dylan McKim sat down for a 1-on-1 interview with Talarico on Sunday. This is the third in Nexstar’s series of profiles of the candidates running to be the next U.S. Senator from Texas. The following transcript is only edited for clarity and readability.

Sit-down with State. Rep. James Talarico

Dylan McKim: I want to really dig into who James Talarico is. You were a former teacher here in San Antonio. You served in a Texas house and you are now in seminary school.

That’s what we can read in the articles when you look at James Talarico. But for people who want to dig deeper besides those articles, I’ll give you a chance here to explain to our viewers across this entire state: who is James Talarico?

James Talarico: Well, I’m an eigth-generation Texan. My family’s been here since it was Mexico.
My entire extended family lives in the Round Rock area. My parents, my younger sister, brother-in-law, my aunts and uncles, and cousins and grandparents, and I just became an uncle two-and-a-half years ago for the first time, which is definitely the best title I’ve gotten so far.
And so when I’m not in the legislature, when I’m not at the seminary, when I’m not preaching or campaigning, I’m usually with them — either my immediate family or my extended family.

DM: I want to talk about the seminary. What is that like? What are you doing actively in seminary? What part of the process are you in your studies?

JT: So I am kind of in the middle of my seminary journey. I graduated in May with a Master’s of Theological Studies. But I’m hoping to go back to finish my Masters of Divinity, which is what I need to become an ordained minister in the Presbyterian Church.

DM: Is that your main goal?

JT: Yes. It’s kind of like passing the bar after you go to law school. So, I want to finish seminary and then I want to get ordained so that I can be officially a minister in the Church.

DM: And why is that a big deal for you? Why do you want to be a minister in church?

JT: Well, you know, my granddad was a Baptist preacher. And when I was young, he told me that we follow the two commandments that Jesus gave us: to love God and to love neighbor.

In my public service as a public school teacher here in San Antonio, but also as a public official in the state legislature, that’s how I love my neighbor. Through public policy: bringing down the cost of childcare, the cost of housing, the cost of prescription drugs, I’m trying to make my neighbor’s life a little easier.

But to sustain that difficult work, it has to be rooted in a love of God. And going to church every Sunday is how I do that. But being in the legislature is a tough job, and I realized I needed more of a sturdy spiritual foundation to do that work, and that’s when I made the decision to go to seminary and take my faith to the next level. And it’s been one of the best decisions I’ve ever made.

DM: Has it changed you? Has it changed the way you think about politics or the way you approach a problem or the way you talk to people?

JT: Yes, absolutely. After doing this political work for eight years in the legislature, I’ve come to see how the roots of so many of our problems, political problems, economic problems, social problems, those roots are spiritual. There is a spiritual crisis in this country.

The ties that bind us together are starting to unravel. And that’s going to require a spiritual solution. That’s going to come from our faith leaders, our houses of worship, but it’s also got to come from ordinary Texans rediscovering their connections to their neighbors and learning again how to love everyone in our communities. And I’m trying to do that in my capacity, not just as a public official, but hopefully as a minister.

DM: Before you got here today, one of your staff members was in the middle stage here and was kind of pumping the crowd up, and she said, you know, ‘I want you to turn to your neighbor, someone you didn’t come here with, and say, ‘Hi, why do you support James, get to know each other.’’ And I heard literally somebody next to me say, ‘Oh, it’s like we’re in church.’ Is that the environment you want at these rallies, [do] you want people to feel like they’re at church?

JT: Not quite. Today’s a Sunday. We were at church over this morning at Travis Park United Methodist Church in downtown San Antonio where we were feeding some of our homeless neighbors and then we gathered with those congregants for worship.

But I do think that our politics needs to have a stronger spiritual foundation and that means rediscovering our connections with one another, and learning how to love each other despite our differences and despite our disagreements. That’s the only way a democracy can survive. It’s the only way this American experiment can continue, if we remember that there’s far more that unites us than divides us.

Part of that is building community. And so it’s why we bring people together in real life. The algorithms on our social media platforms divide us and pit us against each other on an hourly basis. And I think gathering together in person in the flesh and blood, and meeting our neighbors can be the antidote to that kind of division that we see online.

DM: How do you connect with somebody who maybe isn’t spiritual, doesn’t have a faith, maybe they don’t view that as an importance in their life. How do you connect with that specific voter?

JT: Well, you know, I found in my experience, most people have a spiritual dimension to their lives.

Even if they’re not religious, or they don’t belong to any organized religion, they still have some kind of practice to help ground them, to keep them centered, and keep them connected with other people. And that’s universal across the world. It’s a fundamental part of the human experience. And I think it needs to be more present in our civic discourse and in our political relationships with each other.

DM: ‘Love thy neighbor,’ that’s a big part of your campaign. You’re doing events where you’re trying to show you’re helping the community out. You’ve been on the campaign trail for a little bit now. I remember back in August, there was a Texas Politics Project poll that came out about James Talarico, and a big number in that poll was 54% didn’t know who you were.

What are you doing to get your name out there? You had the Joe Rogan interview, you had the momentum from the quorum break. I think those were big hits for you, for your name and for your identity. But have you been able to continue with that momentum and grow your name and your brand across the state?

JT: Well, that’s the purpose of the campaign. It’s to go out to every corner of the state and earn people’s trust, earn their respect, and hopefully earn their support. And just in the first three months, we have traveled to every corner of Texas, from Beaumont to El Paso, from Amarillo to Brownsville and everywhere in between.

We’ve been to big cities and small towns. We’ve been to red areas and blue areas alike. And we’re meeting people one-on-one. We’re hearing about their hopes and their dreams and their fears.

My goal is for the campaign to respond to those needs that people have. And so it’s going to be a process. We’re kind of in the middle of this primary campaign. We’ve got a long way to go to the primary. We’ve got a long way to go until the general election. But I’m going to keep working at this.

I’m probably the least well-known candidate in any of these races. I’m the new guy on the block. And I’ve got my work cut out for me to meet more people and build more relationships all over Texas.

DM: I think you and your other Democratic candidates, you have your work cut out to try to get some of these Republican voters in Texas or independent voters to say, ‘we want a Democrat in a statewide position for the first time in three decades.’

Have you been able to meet with registered Republicans? Have you been able to meet with people who voted for Trump in [20]16, [20]20 and [20]24? And have you been able to persuade them? I mean, what are those conversations like? You’re shaking your head like you did.

JT: Well, in fact, many of the people that came here today to our event in San Antonio told me that they are not Democrats. They’re either independents or they’re Republicans. And they were curious to hear my vision and my values. And they’re open to an alternative because
I think I speak for a lot of people in the state on both sides of the aisle that we are fed up with one-party rule.

Whenever you’ve got one party, whether it’s the Republican Party or the Democratic Party in charge at every level, it always leads to extremism and corruption because there’s no competition, there’s no accountability, there’s no check on that power. We’ve had one party rule in this state for 30 years, and we’ve seen the extremism and the corruption that’s produced right here in Texas.

So, I’m trying to campaign, not just as a Democrat, but as a Texan, who wants to see a government that responds to the needs of the people, not just billionaire megadonors.

DM: You said you and your Democratic state lawmakers were scrappy. More scrappy than your national Democratic counterparts.

What are you most proud [of]? It could be policy, it could be debates you had on the House floor. In your time in the State House, what are you most proud of?

JT: That’s a hard question because I’ve been able to accomplish a lot in the state legislature as a Democrat in a Republican-dominated body. I’ve been able to work across the aisle to pass the first ever cap on insulin copays in Texas history. I passed a bill to import cheaper prescription drugs from Canada right here into Texas to save lives across our state. I passed one of the biggest early childhood bills in recent memory and I passed a bill that’s going to bring down housing costs for people all over Texas. So there’s a lot to be proud of in my work as a state legislator.

I want to take those fights — those fights to bring down costs, those fights against billionaire mega donors — I want to take those fights to our U.S. Senate so that we can finally have a federal government that works for us and not just the ultra wealthy.

DM: Yeah, I want to talk about that. If you do get elected to represent Texas in the U.S. Senate, what’s the day one issue you’re going after? Like you hit the ground running up there in D.C., what do you want to accomplish?

JT: I’ve said that the first bill I would file is a national ban to end gerrymandering once and for all. Because I think this corruption at the heart of our political system is what’s preventing us from making progress on health care, on education, on housing, on bringing down costs at the grocery store. And so we’ve got to tackle that corruption head-on.

Taking big money out of our politics, banning super PACs, banning billionaires from making unlimited secret donations, banning members of Congress from trading stocks and enriching themselves and banning gerrymandering so we can have free and fair elections all across Texas and all across the country. That’s going to be my top priority because I think that’s the biggest problem we face in this country.

DM: I think you even told me this once, back when we were covering the state [legislature], you said, or maybe it’s just a general statement that Republicans and Democrats at the state level, you guys still seem to get along. You still have lunches and dinners with each other, your families know each other, you’re friends, you still have some sort of working relationship. At the national level, it appears we have hit an era where Democrats are with the Democrats, and Republicans are with the Republicans, and there’s a very clear divide.

So you’re coming from a one-party rule state, and you were to be elected, you’d be a Democrat in the Senate. How would you try to bridge the gap up there? How would you try to get Republicans to work with you?

JT: Well, I don’t think you should ever believe something a politician says, I think you should look at what a politician has done. And I’ve served for eight years in the state legislature in Texas. I have a public voting record. I have a public record on what bills I’ve filed and what bills I’ve passed. I have a public record on the debates I’ve had on the House floor and the questions I’ve asked in the committee.

So I encourage all Texans to take a very close look at that record because what they’re going to find is that I’ve been able to stand up to my Republican colleagues when I believe that they’re hurting my constituents, are hurting people all over the state of Texas. When they’re trying to defund public education, when they’re trying to rob people of their health care. But then I’m also able to work across the aisle when we do agree, and pass major legislation to bring down the cost of housing and childcare and prescription drugs.

So we need a legislator who can do both of those things and good leaders can do both. You can fight back and you can get stuff done. That’s what I’ve already been able to do as a legislator and it’s what I want to do as a U.S. Senator.

DM: You mentioned on the stage here that you wanted to eliminate trillionaires — you want to tax the rich. You want to use that money to pay for health care, housing and education. Could you give us more of a plan that you have in place? Do you have a plan for that?

JT: Yeah, we have to have a tax code where billionaires — and I guess trillionaires, because that’s now the world we live in — are paying their fair share so that all of us can succeed and prosper and grow together.

That’s what we had in the 20th century and when we had an economy where we have the largest middle class in American history. That middle class is disappearing as we speak. The top 1% in this country now owns more wealth than the entire middle class.

Three guys: Elon Musk, Mark Zuckerberg, and Jeff Bezos, own more wealth than 165 million Americans combined. And that’s an unacceptable amount of inequality. And so my goal is to restructure this economy so that we can support working-class Texans and middle-class Texans more than we support billionaires and trillionaires.

I think most Texans want to see that kind of economy where people can actually live the American dream. I read a stat that 90% of baby boomers went on to earn more money than their parents. For millennials like me, it’s 50%. For Gen Z, it’s even lower. So the American dream is literally slipping out of reach for most young people in this country. I want to fight for that dream, revitalize it so it can be a reality for more Americans and more Texans.

DM: What’s your stance on immigration?

JT: I think Texans understand immigration better than most people around the country, because we live with the consequences. We know the challenges and we know the benefits of it.

I’ve said that I think our southern border should be like our front porch. There should be a giant welcome mat out front and a lock on the door. Those aren’t mutually exclusive goals.

We can welcome immigrants who want to come here and contribute and live the American dream and make our communities stronger and richer. And we can also have a system that keeps out people who mean to do us harm.

People have a right to know who’s coming into our country. And the failures on our southern border have opened the door to this extremism that we’re currently seeing — masked men in unmarked vehicles kidnapping people off the street, tearing parents from their children. They’re waiting in school pickup lines. They’re waiting in hospital waiting rooms. And so we should be cracking down on the cartels, not our communities. We should be deporting gang members, not moms and babies.

I think that is what most Texans and most Americans want to see — they want to see a policy that’s pro-immigrant and pro-security. We don’t have that right now. Both political parties have failed us on this issue. I plan on fighting for that kind of middle ground if I’m elected as U.S. Senator.

DM: What does a middle ground look like? Does that [mean] continue building the border wall? We’re in the middle of Operation Lone Star, and the governor told me a couple weeks ago that’s not going to stop anytime soon, at least not during the Trump era. So what does that look like security-wise?

JT: I think it looks like comprehensive immigration reform, which is more border security, more border patrol agents reforming our ports of entry so that we can modernize these entry points and detect the fentanyl that’s coming into this country. 93% of the fentanyl that gets into America comes through the ports of entry, but we don’t have the resources or the manpower or the technology to stop it. That’s something that we could do at the federal level.

I actually worked with Republicans in the state house to respond to the crisis we’re seeing in Texas of fentanyl overdoses. I passed a bill to equip every school in Texas with Narcan, which is a medication that can reverse fentanyl overdoses. This is an issue I’ve cared a lot about.

We also need to reform our asylum system. We have to relieve the visa backlog. We’ve got to hire way more immigration judges so we can process these cases, so there’s not this kind of build up in our system. All of those things I think will make us stronger and safer at the end of the day.

DM: I want to talk about this primary we’re in. We know you’re going up against Colin Allred. You may be going up against Jasmine Crockett, that’s to be determined. But how do you differentiate yourself from either Allred, or for multiple candidates in this democratic race to be this standalone candidate?

[Editor’s note: this interview took place the day before Allred stepped out of the race and Crockett officially announced her candidacy for Senate.]

JT: Well, I’m not interested in tearing down my fellow Democrats. I am out here trying to make my case for why I think I’m the best nominee going into the November election.

I first got elected to the state house by flipping a Trump district that no one thought was winnable. I did that by building a big tent of Democrats, independents, and some Republicans. I want to do that at the state level. I also have a track record of getting stuff done in the state legislature. Fighting back against Republican extremism, but also passing major bills to bring down costs for working Texans.

I think that kind of track record, both electorally and legislatively, is exactly what people want to see from a Democratic nominee, and ultimately what Texans want to see from the U.S. Senator.

DM: You talked a lot about your upbringing and your mom today. What does she think of you running for the U.S. Senate?

JT: You know, I think she’s proud of the campaign that we’re running. The reason I ran for the legislature was to help working people like my mom, like my students, and I’ve been able to do that as a state representative. I think I can do that as a US senator. And my mom was here today at the event. She’s been able to join us at some of the stops on the campaign trail. And sometimes she worries about me working too hard, but I think she’s really proud of the campaign we put together.

DM: Is she sharing any ideas with you?

JT: Always. Mom’s always got ideas.

DM: Any ideas you’re implementing?

JT: I think what we’ve tried to do in our policy platform around paid family leave comes directly from her experience as a working mom who didn’t have the ability to take time off from work to spend with me or my sister. That policy proposal that I’m pushing for comes directly from her experience.

DM: What can you tell Democratic voters and independent voters that you’re the guy to break the streak and you’re the guy that’s going to be the first Democrat in 30 years to win a statewide position?

JT: Well again, you shouldn’t listen to what I say. You should look at what I’ve done. I was able to flip the district that Democrats hadn’t won in 30 years. And that’s exactly the kind of track record I bring to this race.

We’re here in San Antonio. The reason I got into politics is because of my students that I’ve taught here on the west side of the city. I entered the classroom in fall of 2011, right after the state legislature cut $5 billion from our schools. I saw students look at the cracks. I saw students fall behind because of those cuts.

In one class, I had 45 kids in one classroom. And there weren’t enough desks for all those kids, so I had students sitting on the air conditioning unit. In the richest country in the world, in the 21st century, children are sitting on the air conditioning unit. And that got me mad.

And it’s why I stepped up as a 28-year-old and ran for office for the first time, and it’s why I’m running for this U.S. Senate seat. Because my students, students just like them, working people all over the state need a champion in the U.S. Senate, and I want to be that champion.

DM: Anything else you want to add, sir?

JT: I don’t think so.

DM: Are you being noticed more? Can you go out?

JT: Yeah, yes. Yeah, I guess it’s just a byproduct of running around the state and having your name all over the place.

DM: Well, I guess it’s a good sign that your name identity is rising..

JT: I guess so.

DM: Well, that’s all I have for you. Thank you so much. I appreciate your time.

data.title: Trump predicts Indiana Senate GOP leader will lose next primary over redistricting vote
data.description:
President Trump predicted that Indiana state Senate Republican leader Rodric Bray will lose his next primary following a failed vote in the chamber to pass a GOP-favored House map on Thursday. 

“I heard he was against it. He’ll probably lose his next primary whenever that is. I hope he does because he’s done a tremendous disservice,” Trump told reporters in the Oval Office on Thursday when asked about the chamber’s rejection of the map. 

Bray is not up for reelection until 2028. 

The president’s comments came shortly after the upper chamber voted against the map on a 19-31 vote. The vote represents a clear act of defiance against Trump who has been pushing for Republican state legislatures to redistrict in an effort to boost the party ahead of next year’s midterm elections. 

The result was not completely unexpected given that various Indiana Republicans had voiced skepticism about redrawing the map. 

Trump’s allies immediately hit back against the vote, saying they would follow through on that threat to challenge GOP holdouts.

“The spineless RINOs in Indiana, many hailing from districts where President Trump won by over 20% just last November have stabbed their own voters in the back and sold out America!” Trump adviser Alex Bruesewitz wrote on the social platform X, responding to the vote.

“We’ll be launching primary challenges against every last traitor who voted no, effective immediately! Pack your bags, your time is up!”

Speaking to reporters, House Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) said it was “disappointing,” but added he was “very, very bullish” about next year’s elections despite Indiana’s rejection of a new map.

“We have better candidates, better message, and we have a lot to show the people, so we’ll continue our momentum,” he said.

data.title: Watch live: Trump gives remarks at White House Congressional Ball
data.description:
President Trump will give remarks Thursday evening at the White House as he hosts the Congressional Ball.

The event comes after the Senate struck down dueling GOP and Democratic health care plans that aimed to address rising insurance premiums exacerbated by expiring subsidies under the Affordable Care Act.

Construction is also ongoing on Trump’s $300 million ballroom after demolition of the East Wing began in October. The project, which is funded by private donation, has stirred some controversy.

The event in the White House Grand Foyer is scheduled to begin at 8:15 p.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: Johnson says he’s ’bullish’ about midterms despite Indiana redistricting loss
data.description:
House Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) on Thursday said he is “bullish” on next year’s midterms in spite of a redistricting loss in Indiana.

“I guess that’s a disappointing outcome in Indiana, but there’s about 14 states at some stage of the process now, either litigation or redistricting. So, we shall see. We shall see. But I’m very, very bullish about the midterms. I know that we are going to win because we have a great record to run on,” Johnson told The Hill on Thursday.

On Thursday, the Indiana Senate did not pass a new House map favoring Republicans, a loss for President Trump after he had pressed state lawmakers to approve the new lines. The upper chamber rejected the map in a 19-31 vote, resulting in applause from some in the Senate.

“We have better candidates, better message and we have a lot to show the people, so we’ll continue our momentum,” Johnson said.

President Trump on Wednesday had refreshed a warning to primary Republican lawmakers that are against the Republican push to switch up Indiana’s congressional map.

“Anybody that votes against Redistricting, and the SUCCESS of the Republican Party in D.C., will be, I am sure, met with a MAGA Primary in the Spring,” Trump said on his social platform Truth Social post.

“If Republicans will not do what is necessary to save our Country, they will eventually lose everything to the Democrats,” he added.

Democrats, who struggled to pull themselves together after losses in the 2024 elections, have widened their House and state legislature target lists in the wake of multiple notable wins and overperformances in the last few months.

Emily Brooks contributed to this report.

data.title: Trump to sign order blocking state AI laws
data.description:
President Trump signed Thursday night an executive order to impose a national AI standard, a move that would seemingly limit states’ efforts to enact their own AI laws.

“We have to be unified. China is unified because they have one vote and that’s President Xi [Xinping],” Trump said, speaking in the Oval Office. “We have a different system, but we have a system that’s good. But we only have a system that’s good if it’s smart.” 

White House AI and crypto czar David Sacks, who joined the president in the Oval alongside Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick and Sen. Ted Cruz (R-Texas), said the order would give the administration “tools to push back on the most onerous and excessive state regulations.” 

However, Sacks suggested they would not fight all state AI laws, pointing to kids’ safety measures as an example. 

Trump hinted at the order Monday, arguing in a Truth Social post that there should be “only One Rulebook if we are going to continue to lead in AI.”

The president initially appeared to be considering an order last month, as House Republican leaders faced pushback amid a renewed effort to pass a measure preempting state AI laws. 

A draft order seen by The Hill would have created a task force dedicated to fighting state AI measures in court and restricted federal broadband funding for states whose AI laws are deemed overly burdensome. 

GOP leaders reportedly urged the president to hold off, as they sought to include a preemption provision in the National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA). However, the issue faced resistance from several Republicans, including Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene (Ga.), Sen. Josh Hawley (Mo.) and Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis. 

It was ultimately not included in the final legislation, with House Majority Leader Steve Scalise (R-La.) saying last week that they were “looking at other places” for the measure. 

This is the second time that Republicans lawmakers have attempted to include such a provision in key legislation. A 10-year moratorium on state AI laws was included in Trump’s tax and spending bill earlier this year but was ultimately stripped out by the Senate. 

The Trump White House and its Silicon Valley allies have argued that preemption is necessary to prevent a patchwork of state AI laws that could disrupt innovation amid fierce competition with China. 

However, state lawmakers contend that they are better positioned to regulate the rapidly evolving technology, especially given Congress’s previous struggles to pass legislation on social media and privacy.  

Updated at 7:05 p.m. EST

data.title: Senate ACA votes fail
